item.title: The Real NBA Season Starts Now: Inside the Summer of Free Agency
item.body:
## The trophy is handed out, and the chaos begins

The confetti from the NBA Finals has barely been swept up, but for front offices and fans, the most frenzied stretch of the basketball calendar is just beginning. Late June and early July are when championship windows open and slam shut — not on the court, but in conference rooms and over late-night phone calls.

## The offseason's three big levers

Reshaping a roster runs on a tight, dramatic timeline built around a few key moments:

- **The NBA Draft**, where the league's next generation gets its first jerseys and rebuilding teams place their bets on the future.
- **Free agency**, when veterans and rising stars choose where to sign, often reshaping the balance of power overnight.
- **The trade market**, a quieter but constant churn of deals that can be even more consequential than the headline signings.

## Why this summer carries extra weight

The modern NBA's financial rules have made roster-building a high-stakes puzzle. Tightened spending restrictions on the biggest-payroll teams mean that every contract decision ripples outward. A single max signing can lock a franchise into a multi-year identity — or trigger a cascade of cost-cutting trades to stay compliant. Teams that nail the math give themselves years of flexibility; teams that miss can spend seasons digging out.

## What fans should watch for

Beyond the marquee names, the smartest moves are often the quiet ones: a savvy mid-level signing, a draft-night trade that nets future picks, or a young player re-signed before the rest of the league realizes his value. Dynasties and disappointments are usually decided in these weeks, long before opening night.

## The takeaway

The beauty of the NBA offseason is that hope is universal. Every fan base, regardless of how the last season ended, gets to imagine a better tomorrow. For a few electric weeks each summer, the standings are blank, the rumors are flying, and every team is — at least on paper — still in the race.
